title: Chapter 5: The Wait
content: The forty-eight hours after Chloe sent the email were the longest of her life.

Not because she was afraid. Fear was a luxury she'd burned through years ago, somewhere between her first viral takedown and her third therapist who'd tried to convince her that anger was a symptom. Fear required believing you had something to lose, and Chloe had been systematically stripped of everything worth losing before she even arrived at Serenity.
No, the waiting was hard because it was boring.

Institution time was different from real time. In real time, forty-eight hours was a weekend — two nights out, three meals you chose yourself, an indeterminate number of TikToks. In institution time, forty-eight hours was an eternity of scheduled activities designed to fill the space between medication rounds. Yoga at nine. Group at eleven. Mindfulness walk at two. Journaling at four. Dinner at six. Medication at eight.
The same thing, every day, on a loop so tight it made Groundhog Day look like improv theater.
Chloe kept herself sane by tracking anomalies.

Day five, 8:15 AM: Nurse Holloway paused two seconds longer than usual at Chloe's door during morning rounds. Her eyes swept the room — the bed, the plant, the journal on the desk — before moving on. Chloe had already moved the pills from the plant spine to a new hiding spot: the hollow plastic casing inside the desk lamp's base, unscrewable with a thumbnail.

Day five, 2:30 PM: Dr. Sterling made an unscheduled appearance in the common room. He almost never came to the common room. His domain was the second floor — the control room, the admin offices, the leather chair in his office that probably cost more than Chloe's entire education. But here he was, standing in the doorway in his six-thousand-dollar suit, surveying the patients like a landlord checking on a property.
His eyes landed on Chloe for exactly three seconds. She felt it like a finger on her spine.
Then he left.
"They know something," Chloe told Layla in the laundry room that night.

"They don't know anything," Layla said, her voice muffled by the dryer roar. "If they knew, we'd already be in isolation. Sterling's just paranoid. You sent an email from inside his fortress. Even if he hasn't found it, he can feel something's off. It's like..." She searched for the right word. "A draft. A door open somewhere he can't locate."
"Can he trace it back to us?"
"Not from the email alone. I routed it through three internal relays. By the time anyone traces it, it'll look like it came from the cafeteria workstation."
"The cafeteria has a workstation?"
"It has a terminal for the kitchen inventory system. Old Linux box. Nobody's patched it since 2019." Layla's smile was small and sharp. "It's practically a welcome mat."
Chloe filed that away. The cafeteria workstation. Another tool. Another angle.
"How long until we know if the reporter picked it up?"
Layla's fingers paused on the tablet. "That's the problem. I can send. I can't receive. We won't know if anyone responds until we see it on the news — or until Sterling starts panicking."
"So we wait."
"We wait."
But waiting, Chloe learned quickly, didn't mean doing nothing.

Maya turned out to be a better strategist than any of them had expected. On day six, she pulled Chloe aside after morning yoga — the session where they were supposed to be "connecting with their bodies" while an orderly watched from a folding chair in the corner.
"You're thinking about this like a hack," Maya said. "Digital. Information. Outbound."
"That's because it is a hack."

"No. It's a siege." Maya's voice was low, her eyes tracking the orderly's position without appearing to. "You've fired one shot over the wall. But sieges aren't won by a single arrow. They're won by making the people inside the castle realize they're outnumbered."
Chloe stopped walking. "You want to recruit more patients."

"I want to recruit the ones who've been here longest. The ones who know things you can't learn from mapping floor plans." Maya's jaw set. "There's a woman on the second floor — Rachel. Been here three years. She was a psychiatrist before she got committed. Her crime was publishing a paper about institutional abuse in private psychiatric facilities."
"They committed a psychiatrist for writing about psychiatric abuse."
"They committed a Black woman for exposing the industry that employs them." Maya's eyes were hard. "The rules are different depending on who's breaking them."

Chloe absorbed that. It wasn't a new idea — she'd spent her entire adult life documenting exactly this dynamic — but hearing it from Maya, who'd lived it in a body that the world read as a threat before it read anything else, landed differently.
"Can we reach Rachel?"
"Second floor is restricted. Only patients with 'advanced treatment compliance' get access." Maya's mouth twisted. "Translation: patients who've been here long enough to stop fighting."
"So Rachel stopped fighting."
"Rachel learned to look like she stopped fighting." Maya's eyes met Chloe's. "Sound familiar?"
Chloe filed Rachel under: *assets, unconfirmed, second floor.*
Day seven brought the first sign that the flare had been seen.
It didn't come through email. It came through the orderly rotation.

Rodriguez — the night guard who called his girlfriend at 2:15 AM — was replaced. No warning, no explanation. The new night guard was a woman Chloe had never seen before: mid-forties, military posture, eyes that moved like security cameras.
Sophia noticed it first. Of course she did.
"Rodriguez is gone," she whispered at breakfast, her spoon hovering over her oatmeal without moving. "Replaced by someone who looks like she's killed people."
"You don't know that."

"I know body language." Sophia's voice didn't waver. "I've been studying the staff for eight months. Rodriguez slouched. He checked his phone. He was bored. This woman? She's not bored. She's hunting."

Holloway's office was beige, like everything else, but the beige here was different. It was the beige of authority — the desk, the framed certifications, the locked cabinet that Chloe was certain contained files on every patient. Not medical files. The other kind. The kind that explained why each girl was really here.
"Chloe." Holloway gestured to the chair across from her desk. "Sit."
Chloe sat. She'd learned, by now, which battles to fight and which to let pass. Sitting was a let-pass.
"How are you feeling?"
"Fine."
"Just fine?"
"Fine is the goal, isn't it? You're trying to make us fine."

Holloway's smile didn't move, but something behind it shifted — the same micro-flinch Chloe had caught on day one. She'd gotten better at reading it. The flinch meant *this is not going how I planned.*
"We've noticed you've been... social," Holloway said. "Making connections. That's good. Community is an important part of the healing process."
Chloe waited.

"But we've also noticed some concerning patterns." Holloway opened a file on her desk — Chloe's file, presumably. "You've been skipping Group B consistently. You spend a lot of time in the laundry room after hours. And your journal — we've reviewed it — shows very little engagement with the therapeutic prompts."
"I've been journaling in my own way."
"We have a curriculum, Chloe. The prompts are designed to guide your recovery."
Chloe leaned back in her chair. "Can I ask you something?"
Holloway's eyebrows rose — a permission the patients rarely requested.
"What exactly am I recovering from?"

Holloway's face went very still. Not the performative stillness of her professional mask. Something underneath it — the real person, the one who'd been in this business for thirty years, the one who thought she'd seen every variation of defiance a young woman could produce.
She'd never seen this one.
"That sounds like a threat," Holloway said.
"It's not a threat." Chloe opened the door. "It's a promise. There's a difference."
She stepped into the hallway. Behind her, she heard the scratch of Holloway's pen on paper. She didn't need to see the note to know what it said.
*Patient escalating. Recommend increased observation.*
Let them observe. Let them write their notes. Let them tighten security and replace orderlies and call in professionals who looked like they'd killed people.

None of it mattered anymore, because the flare was out. Somewhere in the world beyond the redwoods, a reporter was reading their story. And Chloe had learned something in that office that Holloway didn't know she'd revealed:
They were scared.

The institution with the 94% success rate, the compliance factory that had processed hundreds of inconvenient daughters, the machine that ran on schedules and medication and the assumption that every girl could be broken — was scared of four women who'd stopped believing they were sick.
Chloe walked back to Room 214, past the new night guard with the military posture, past the cameras with their red heartbeats, past the beige walls and the lavender-scented air.
She lay on her bed and stared at the ceiling.
"We sent the flare," she whispered. "They saw it. Now they're scared."
The camera blinked.
"Good."
